content.problemStatement:
The Little Elephant likes permutations. This time he has a permutation **A[1]**, **A[2]**, ..., **A[N]** of numbers **1**, **2**, ..., **N**.



He calls a permutation **A** good, if the number of its inversions is equal to the number of its local inversions. The number of inversions is equal to the number of pairs of integers (**i**; **j**) such that **1** ≤ **i** < **j** ≤ **N** and **A[i]** > **A[j]**, and the number of local inversions is the number of integers **i** such that **1** ≤ **i** < **N** and **A[i]** > **A[i+1]**.



The Little Elephant has several such permutations. Help him to find for each permutation whether it is good or not. Print **YES** for a corresponding test case if it is good and **NO** otherwise.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the size of the permutation), the second line contains N space‑separated integers A[1] … A[N] forming a permutation of 1…N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the permutation is good (the number of global inversions equals the number of local inversions) or "NO" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: N=1, no inversions, condition holds. Case 2: permutation [2,1] has each element at distance ≤1, so YES. Case 3: [3,2,1] has element 3 at position1 (distance 2), thus NO. Case 4: [1,3,2,4] satisfies the distance condition, so YES.
All elements are at most one position away from their sorted place, therefore global and local inversions coincide.
Element 3 is two positions away from its correct index, creating a non‑local global inversion.

solution.approach: A permutation has the same number of global inversions as local inversions iff every element is at most one position away from its sorted position. In 1‑based indexing this means for each i (1 ≤ i ≤ N) |A[i] - i| ≤ 1. We simply scan the array and check this condition. If any element violates it, answer is "NO", otherwise "YES". This runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(T·N).
